record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (county-level from territorial period; statewide vital records registration began 1920)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1920 (births/deaths statewide registration; compliance spotty until late 1920s)

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — New Mexico; SRCA county records guides; no officially designated 'burned counties' in NM, though some records lost to deterioration, Civil War incidents, and the 1967 Tierra Amarilla courthouse raid
administrative_notes: New Mexico became a US territory in 1850 following the Mexican-American War and achieved statehood January 6, 1912, as the 47th state. The region was under Spanish rule from 1598 and Mexican rule from 1821 to 1846. Nine original counties were created in 1852. NM's genealogical records are uniquely layered: Spanish colonial (1598-1821), Mexican (1821-1846), US territorial (1850-1912), and statehood periods each produced distinct record types. Catholic parish records, often older than civil records, are primary sources for Hispanic and Pueblo families. The NM State Records Center and Archives (SRCA) in Santa Fe is the central repository for colonial archives (SANM I & II, MANM), territorial records, and land grant files. NM has 19 Pueblos, the Navajo Nation, and the Mescalero and Jicarilla Apache reservations — each with distinct record-keeping traditions. Land grants (mercedes) under Spanish and Mexican law are a unique and complex record type.

core_repositories.source_name: New Mexico State Records Center and Archives (SRCA)
core_repositories.description: Central repository for NM historical records. Houses the Spanish Archives of New Mexico (SANM I — civil/military records, SANM II — land grants), Mexican Archives of NM (MANM), territorial government records, district court records, land grant files, and the Court of Private Land Claims records. Also holds microfilmed county records, governor's papers, and vital records. The most important single repository for NM genealogy.

Major research library with manuscript collections, oral histories, photograph archives, and published genealogies relating to NM and the Southwest. Holdings include land grant research materials, Hispanic genealogies, Native American collections, and the John Donald Robb Archive of Southwestern Music.

Named for the pioneering NM genealogist, this research library holds manuscripts, maps, photographs, and published genealogies. Father Chavez's Origins of New Mexico Families is the foundational work for colonial-era Hispanic genealogy.
Southern NM and border region collections including ranch records, Mesilla Valley manuscripts, mining records, and oral histories. Essential for Dona Ana, Sierra, Grant, and Luna county research.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Catholic parish records (some from 1600s), vital records, county records, military records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively from the Archdiocese of Santa Fe and SRCA.
Wiki guide to county-level records with links to digitized microfilm for individual counties.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic NM newspapers in both English and Spanish. Includes territorial-era papers from the 1840s onward — Santa Fe New Mexican (1849), Albuquerque Journal, Las Vegas Optic, and others. Legal notices, land grant proceedings, obituaries.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: UNM Digital Repository — New Mexico Newspapers
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Additional digitized NM newspapers and periodicals not in Chronicling America. Includes small-town papers, Spanish-language press, and tribal newspapers.

native_american_and_removal_records.description: Tribal enrollment records, vital records, and census data for the Navajo Nation, which extends across NW New Mexico (San Juan, McKinley, Cibola counties). The Navajo Nation is the largest tribal nation in the US with over 300,000 enrolled members. Navajo historical records include the Long Walk period (1863-1868), Treaty of 1868 records, and livestock reduction era documents.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: 19 Pueblos of New Mexico — Tribal & BIA Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Individual Pueblos / BIA Northern & Southern Pueblos Agencies / NARA Denver
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Each of NM's 19 Pueblos (Acoma, Cochiti, Isleta, Jemez, Kewa/Santo Domingo, Laguna, Nambe, Ohkay Owingeh, Picuris, Pojoaque, San Felipe, San Ildefonso, Sandia, Santa Ana, Santa Clara, Taos, Tesuque, Zia, Zuni) maintains its own governmental records. BIA census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school records, and agency correspondence are at NARA Denver. Spanish colonial mission records at Pueblos date to 1598-1680 (pre-Pueblo Revolt) and 1692+ (post-reconquest).

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Mescalero Apache Tribe, Mescalero / NARA Denver
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Tribal enrollment and BIA records for the Mescalero Apache Reservation in Lincoln/Otero counties. Includes records of the Bosque Redondo internment (1863-1868), allotment records, and agency correspondence.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Jicarilla Apache Nation, Dulce / NARA Denver
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Tribal records for the Jicarilla Apache Reservation in Rio Arriba and Sandoval counties. BIA Jicarilla Agency records at NARA include census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: US-Mexico Border Crossing Records — NM Districts
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Border crossing manifests for NM ports of entry including Columbus, Deming, Lordsburg, and El Paso district (covering Dona Ana County). Essential for tracing Mexican immigrant families who crossed into southern NM from the 1890s to 1950s.

Territorial and state-era naturalization records from NM district courts. Declarations of intention and petitions for citizenship. Also includes Mexican-era citizenship documentation for families who transitioned from Mexican to US sovereignty.

military_records.description: State military records including NM National Guard records, WWI and WWII service records, Bataan Death March records (NM lost more soldiers per capita at Bataan than any other state), and territorial militia records. Also holds Spanish colonial military records (SANM I).

Records from NM military installations: Fort Union, Fort Stanton, Fort Selden, Fort Bayard, Fort Wingate, Fort Sumner/Bosque Redondo, Kirtland AFB, Holloman AFB, White Sands, Cannon AFB, and Los Alamos. Post returns, personnel records, and installation records.

land_and_property.source_name: Bureau of Land Management — General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims) for NM. Searchable by name, county, and land description. Important for eastern NM homesteading counties (Roosevelt, Curry, Quay, Lea, etc.) and mining claim patents throughout the territory.

New Mexico's land grant system is unique in American law. SANM II contains original Spanish and Mexican land grant documents. The Surveyor General's records and Court of Private Land Claims (1891-1904) adjudication files document the US government's review of all Spanish/Mexican era grants. Over 150 grants were filed; approximately 46 were confirmed. These records are essential for tracing colonial NM families.

vital_records.source_name: New Mexico Vital Records — Department of Health
vital_records.description: State-level vital records: births and deaths from 1920 (statewide registration). Compliance was poor in early years. Pre-1920, check county clerk records and Catholic parish registers. Historical records available through SRCA for older periods.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Archdiocese of Santa Fe — Parish Records
church_and_cemetery.description: The Archdiocese of Santa Fe oversees Catholic parish records spanning four centuries. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively from archdiocesan archives — parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) from missions and parishes across NM, some dating to the 1690s (post-Pueblo Revolt reconquest). These are the single most important genealogical source for Hispanic NM families.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Find A Grave — New Mexico Cemeteries
church_and_cemetery.description: Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for NM. Coverage includes historic camposantos (community cemeteries), national cemeteries (Santa Fe National Cemetery), and church burial grounds.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes quarterly journal and maintains databases. Hosts annual conference and provides research assistance for NM families.
Comprehensive research guide covering NM record types, repositories, county formation history, and research strategies. Includes links to digitized records and county-specific pages.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, family files, and county histories.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Hispanic Genealogical Research Center of New Mexico
research_guides_and_societies.description: Specialized in Hispanic genealogy of NM. Maintains databases, offers research guidance, and publishes materials on colonial NM families. Partners with SRCA and the Archdiocese.

historical_counties_extinct.description: New Mexico has no extinct counties, but the nine original territorial counties (1852) — Bernalillo, Dona Ana, Rio Arriba, San Miguel, Santa Ana (renamed Sandoval area), Santa Fe, Socorro, Taos, and Valencia — were vastly larger than today's boundaries. Twenty-four additional counties were created between 1860 and 1981 (Cibola, the most recent). Arizona Territory was separated from NM in 1863. Researchers must trace county formation dates carefully to identify which parent county held records before a daughter county was created. The SRCA maintains county formation charts.

burned_counties_summary.notes: New Mexico has no counties officially classified as 'burned counties' in the genealogical sense. However, some record losses have occurred: the 1862 Civil War burning of Mora town caused minor damage; the 1967 Tierra Amarilla courthouse raid in Rio Arriba County damaged some records; and natural deterioration has affected some Spanish colonial documents. Despite these incidents, NM counties retain substantially complete records from their formation dates. Spanish colonial records (1598-1821) have varying levels of survival, with the best-preserved collections at SRCA and in Catholic parish archives.
